statement: Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir presented a parliamentary resolution (stjórnartillaga til þingsályktunar) on 9 March 2026 proposing a referendum on 29 August 2026 with the question: 'Á að halda áfram viðræðum um aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu?' (Should negotiations on Iceland's EU accession continue?). In her flutningsræða (sponsor's speech), she framed the referendum as a sovereignty issue — arguing that allowing the nation to decide is itself an exercise of sovereignty. She drew parallels with three historical decisions (NATO membership, EFTA accession, EEA agreement) that were also contentious at the time but are now widely regarded as beneficial. She stated that Iceland's 2009 EU application remains valid, confirmed by the EU itself, and that negotiations could resume 'around the turn of the year' if the public votes yes. She explicitly pledged: 'Ég mun aldrei skrifa undir samning sem tryggir ekki okkar markmið og okkar yfirráð' (I will never sign a deal that does not secure our goals and our sovereignty) — specifically regarding fisheries. She cited precedents for special arrangements: Nordic agricultural exemptions (Sweden, Finland) and property purchase restrictions (Denmark, Malta).
source_name: Alþingi — Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir, utanríkisráðherra (flutningsræða, 157. löggjafarþing)
caveats: This is a political speech presenting the government's position, not a neutral analysis. The claim that negotiations could start 'around the turn of the year' is aspirational. The precedents cited (Nordic agriculture, Maltese property) were negotiated under different circumstances and may not directly apply to Icelandic fisheries, which is a far larger share of the economy.

related_entries.statement: The coalition government formed in late 2024, comprising Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), agreed in their coalition agreement to hold a referendum on whether to resume EU accession negotiations by 2027. The referendum was subsequently scheduled for 29 August 2026. The question asks whether Iceland should resume accession negotiations with the EU — not whether to join. This is the first Icelandic government since Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (2009–2013) to actively pursue EU-related policy. Prime Minister Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin) and Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (Viðreisn) have been the primary government figures advancing the referendum. Previous governments, including those led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson (2013–2016) and Katrín Jakobsdóttir (2017–2024), did not pursue EU accession despite intermittent public calls for a referendum.

After the 2013 Icelandic parliamentary elections, the new coalition government of the Progressive Party and Independence Party sought to withdraw Iceland's EU membership application. In February 2015, the governing parties submitted a bill to Althingi seeking approval to formally withdraw, but the bill failed due to vigorous opposition obstruction and was never put to a final vote. Foreign Minister Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson then unilaterally sent a letter to the EU on 12 March 2015, stating that Iceland should no longer be regarded as a candidate country. The EU's response was ambiguous — the European Commission's spokesperson stated that Sveinsson's letter did not constitute a formal withdrawal of the application. The Latvian EU presidency replied on behalf of the Council. The decision to bypass parliament provoked significant public protests outside the Althingi. The EU has continued to regard Iceland's 2009 application as technically still valid, a point that became relevant again in the lead-up to the 2026 referendum.

Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU) provides that any member state may decide to withdraw from the Union in accordance with its own constitutional requirements. The withdrawal process involves a two-year negotiation period, extendable by unanimous agreement of the European Council.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Ríkisstjórnin lofaði að fara með ESB-málið í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu eigi síðar en 2027.

Þingsályktunartillaga ríkisstjórnarinnar snýst um fullt forræði yfir auðlindum þjóðarinnar, en í tillögunni er talað um "forræði" en ekki "fullt forræði". Forsætisráðherra var ekki tilbúinn í dag að lýsa því yfir að fullt forræði væri skilyrði.

citations.report_title: Enginn afsláttur gefinn segi þjóðin já við aðildarviðræðum

claims.claim_text: Þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan um ESB-aðild var kynnt í þingsályktunartillögu sem ríkisstjórnin lagði fram á föstudaginn 6. mars 2026.
Í þingsályktunartillögunni er þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan ákveðin þannig að spurt verður: "Á að halda áfram viðræðum um aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu?"
Þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla um aðildarviðræður er ákvæði í stjórnarsáttmála ríkisstjórnarinnar.
Ísland mun geta greiðt atkvæði aftur um ESB-aðild að loknum aðildarviðræðum, segi þjóðin já í fyrstu þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni.
Þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan er ákveðin þannig að hún er ekki atkvæðagreiðsla um ESB-aðild í heild, heldur um hvort hafist verði handa við aðildarviðræður.
